params.text:
Confidentiality, storage, retention and going electronic

Care records contain sensitive personal information, so they are protected by the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018. You must keep information confidential and share it only with those who need it to support the person — on a “need to know” basis.

  • Secure storage: paper records locked away; electronic records password-protected and never left open on an unattended screen.
  • Access: people have a right to see the records held about them and can request a copy; the provider must respond within one month.
  • Retention: keep records only as long as needed. Adult social care records are generally kept for a minimum of eight years, following your organisation's retention policy.
  • Disposal: dispose securely — shred paper, and delete electronic files properly — never in ordinary bins or recycling.

Many services now use electronic (digital) records. They improve legibility, are easier to share safely and keep an automatic audit trail — but the same principles apply: log in as yourself, never share passwords, and keep entries accurate and timely.

params.alt: A health professional recording care notes on a digital tablet

params.question: Which of these follow the rules on confidentiality, storage and retention? (Select all that apply.)

answers.text: Sharing information only with those who need it to support the person.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The "need to know" principle is central to confidentiality under UK GDPR.

answers.text: Logging in as yourself on the electronic system and never sharing your password.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Your login identifies who made each entry, so it must never be shared.

answers.text: Leaving a paper care file open on the kitchen table between visits.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This breaches confidentiality and secure-storage duties. Records must be kept locked away.

answers.text: Keeping records securely for the retention period, then disposing of them by shredding.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Records are kept for the required period, then destroyed securely rather than binned.
